preamble: The shift from the voiced velar stop [g] to the voiced bilabial stop [b] typically occurs when the original sound is a labialized velar [gʷ] (produced with lip rounding). If a language loses the velar closure but retains the lip rounding, the sound 'slides' forward to become a pure bilabial stop. This is a classic example of labialization.

examples.note: The PIE labiovelar shifted to a bilabial stop in Greek before certain vowels.
The 'gu' cluster labialized into a 'b' in Balkan Romance.
P-Celtic languages systematically shifted labiovelars to labials.
